elements.content: SORN is a fast-paced mecha-action game developed in Unreal Engine 5.6. It is written in C++ and Blueprints. The team consisted of 4 programmers, 3 artists, 1 vfx artist, 3 level designers, 2 UI designers, 1 narrative designer, and 1 sound designer.

elements.alt: Image of BP_PlayerMechCharacter and it's components.

elements.content: The mech character is a subclass of Unreal's character class with additional functionality to support swappable parts, synced animations, and the custom movement states. I made a system that allows Character Movement Component stats to be driven by gameplay attributes, which allows the customization system to interface with many movement related stats.

To support character customization without limiting skeletons, each piece(legs, torso, left arm, right arm) is it's own skeletal mesh. This presented challenges when attempting to sync animations across all the pieces. To solve this, I created 2 solutions. One for one-shot animations, and the other for cutscene authoring.

The tagged animation system associates animation montages with tags. Mech parts can "opt in" to listening for a particular animation. Instead of playing an animation montage on the character mesh, you make a request to play a particular animation tag. Any parts listening for that tag will then play their animation. This is authored using a data asset. This data asset contains all the info required to equip a mech part.

elements.alt: Image of the mech part data asset and tagged animation system.

elements.content: The proxy animation system allows the use of 1 control rig accross all mech parts. This is done using an empty proxy armature that is driven by an Unreal Control Rig. Values from this armature are then gathered at runtime and fed into the animation blueprints of the mech parts individually. This allows cutscene animators to be able to animate the mech without knowing what actual parts are equipped. The proxy armature's bones consist of values like velocity, head aim location, left arm target location, etc.

elements.alt: Image of the control rig editor for the proxy armature.
Image of the control rig in action in sequencer

elements.content: All player abilities (firing, jumping, reloading, repairing, meleeing, etc) are built using the Gameplay Ability System. One ability I am especially proud of is the melee. The melee uses many of the systems built for mech characters, including the camera manager and the synced animation system.

When activated the melee finds a target. It does so first by checking if the player has a target locked, if not, it uses a nearby suitable target. If there are no targets nearby, it uses camera direction and a set distance. The melee animation is then played, and the target snapping behavior is then handled using root motion and motion warping. This gives designers easy control over snapping curves and windows using the Animation Montage Editor.

elements.alt: Image of the animation montage editor for the quad legs. The motion warping notify state is currently selected.

elements.content: Melee hitboxes are implemented using a custom animation notify state. The custom animation notify state allows designers to attach capsule colliders to start and end sockets. It also handle's accounting for duplicating hits. Below is an image of what the authoring process for that looks like. Building this animation state ended up saving lots of time later due to a last minute final boss pivot, allowing some of its attacks to be created incredibly quickly by reusing this system.

elements.alt: Image of the animation montage editor for the left arm of a mech. The melee trace notify state is selected and debug tracing is enabled.

elements.content: Too allow for the dynamic camera motion during abilities like melee, the priority based camera state machine is used. This allows for individual camera states to be defined with a priority, and then evaluated based on the character's owned gameplay tags. The highest priority camera tag will be selected using a max heap. When state tags are added to the pawn, the state gets added to the heap, and they are removed when the tag is removed. This is done using tag added/removed events to prevent unnecessary checks on tick. With this camera state machine, the melee camera was able to be implemented simply by adding the state's associated gameplay tag to the "Ability Owned Tags" section. Below is a code snippet of a camera state definition.

In SORN, the player primarily progresses through the game by completing mission content. To facilitate the development of missions, I created a mission editor system, leveraging Unreal's Editor Graph Tools. Missions are made up primarily of objective chains, objectives, conditions and actions.

elements.alt: Screenshot depicting the mission graph editor.

elements.content: Each objective has a condition and a set of actions. When an active objective's condition is evaluated to true, all actions are executed and the next objective is then activated. To allow for multiple conditions to be used on 1 objective, there are "or" and "and" nodes. Nodes can be selected to edit additional properties. The objective pictured below is configured to spawn a level instance on completion when the player enters a particular radius.

elements.alt: Screenshot depicting the settings available for an objective.

elements.content: Often conditions and actions require a reference to objects in the level. To create these references, gameplay tags are used. A component that can be attached to an actor registers the actor with a particular gameplay tag. Multiple actors can be registered with the same tag if desired. Conditions, actions, and any other gameplay code can than query these tags to bind to actors in the level. Pictured below are the configurable settings of the "Enter Radius" condition. As pictured, the condition activates when the player enters within 2500cm of the actor registered with the "Directives.Missions.TheWall.EnterWall" tag. The condition also has configurable text to control it's UI representation.

When animating some robot characters, I implemented dynamic inverse kinematics to speed up of the pipeline and create animations that properly reacted to gameplay. For example, for the "Hermit" enemy, I move all 4 of their legs using IK. To help make the movement feel more believable, only non-adjacent legs can move at the same time. This IK code was all done in the animation blueprint. In the future I would like to multi-thread what I can from the IK calculations, however this may not be possible due to the traces required. It also may not be necessary for the scale of the game.
As a leg steps it raises based on a curve. All of these evaluated curve values are then averaged for the torso movement.
I built control rigs for varius mech parts and characters in SORN using FK, IK and proxy controls.
